title: - UNIFYING LIKELIHOOD FREE INFERENCE WITH - BLACK BOX OPTIMIZATION AND BEYOND [PDF]

paragraphs.text:
Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## - UNIFYING LIKELIHOOD FREE INFERENCE WITH - BLACK BOX OPTIMIZATION AND BEYOND

**Dinghuai Zhang**[1] _[,]_[2] **, Jie Fu**[1] _[,]_[2] _[∗]_ **, Yoshua Bengio**[1] _[,]_[2] _[,]_[3] **, Aaron Courville**[1] _[,]_[2] _[,]_[3] 1Mila, 2University of Montreal, 3CIFAR Fellow Montreal, Canada _{_ dinghuai.zhang, fujie _}_ @mila.quebec

## ABSTRACT

Black-box optimization formulations for biological sequence design have drawn recent attention due to their promising potential impact on the pharmaceutical industry. In this work, we propose to unify two seemingly distinct worlds: likelihood-free inference and black-box optimization, under one probabilistic framework. In tandem, we provide a recipe for constructing various sequence design methods based on this framework. We show how previous optimization approaches can be “reinvented” in our framework, and further propose new probabilistic black-box optimization algorithms. Extensive experiments on sequence design application illustrate the benefits of the proposed methodology.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Discovering new drugs to fulfill specific criteria, such as binding affinity towards a given molecular target, is a fundamental problem in chemistry and the pharmaceutical industry (Hughes et al., 2011). In this work, we focus on an important subdomain: _de novo_ biological sequence design. This task is challenging for two reasons: (1) the exploration space for sequences is combinatorially large; and (2) sequence usefulness is evaluated via a complicated process which usually involves time-consuming and expensive wet-lab experiments.

Despite the difficulty of this task, many approaches have been developed over the past few decades thanks to recent advances in biochemistry and machine learning. The Nobel Prize wining paradigm, _directed evolution_ (Chen & Arnold, 1991), which conducts local evolutionary search under human guidance, is one of the popular techniques. Unfortunately, it is limited by its sample inefficiency and reliance on strong prior knowledge, _e.g._ , about where to mutate (Ahn et al., 2020). Furthermore, to compete with other machine learning methods (Gottipati et al., 2020), guided evolution (Yoshikawa et al., 2018; Jensen, 2019; Nigam et al., 2019) heavily relies on human intuition for designing domain-specific evolutionary operators, which may not always apply to tasks at hand.

In this work, we deem sequence design to be a black-box optimization problem, tasked with maximizing an unknown oracle function. We assume that oracle queries are limited due to the constraint on resources, such as the budgets for evaluating queries in a wet-lab. Thus, sample efficiency is crucial. We develop a probabilistic framework by reformulating the aforementioned black-box optimization target as a posterior modeling problem. With this framework, we draw a surprising connection between likelihood-free inference and sequence design, and thus linking two fields which are previously considered as unrelated. The key observation we leverage here for establishing this connection is that both settings share similar elements and targets which will be elaborated in Section 2.2. This connection facilitates our understanding of both fields and provides a recipe for developing sequence design algorithms. Going beyond, we also combine different probabilistic modeling insights and develop three novel _composite_ probabilistic algorithms. We point out that our framework could actually be applied to any black-box optimization settings, but in this work we focus on its application to biological sequence design.
To demonstrate the empirical effectiveness of our methods, we conduct systematical experiments to evaluate their performance on four _in-silico_ sequence design benchmarks. Our proposed meth-

> _∗_ Corresponding Author

1

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

ods achieve at least comparable results to existing baselines, and the proposed composite methods behave consistently better than all other ones across various sequence design tasks.

We summarize our contribution as follows:

- We develop a probabilistic framework that unifies likelihood-free inference and black-box optimization.
- Based on this framework, we provide a recipe for designing algorithms for black-box problems. We apply these ideas to propose a series of composite design algorithms.

- We perform systematical evaluation on a series of black-box sequence design benchmarks, and find that these algorithms achieves consistently comparable or better results compared to previous ones, thus illustrating the benefit of the proposed unified framework.

- 2 A UNIFYING PROBABILISTIC FRAMEWORK

- 2.1 BACKGROUND

**Likelihood-free inference (LFI)** . We use _**θ** ∈_ Θ and **x** _∈X_ to separately denote the parameters and the data generated via the mechanism **x** _∼ p_ ( **x** _|_ _**θ**_ ). In this scenario, LFI refers to a special kind of Bayesian inference setting where the likelihood function is not tractable but sampling (by simulation) from the likelihood is feasible. Consider the objective of modeling the Bayesian posterior when we cannot compute the likelihood _p_ ( **x** _o|_ _**θ**_ ):

where **x** _o_ is the observed data, _p_ ( _**θ**_ ) is the (given) prior over the model parameters _**θ**_ , _p_ ( **x** _|_ _**θ**_ ) is the intractable likelihood function and _p_ ( _**θ** |_ **x** ) is the desired posterior over _**θ**_ . While we do not have access to the exact likelihood, we can still simulate (sample) data **x** from the model simulator: **x** _∼ p_ ( **x** _|_ _**θ**_ ). Instead of trying to obtain a numerical value of the generic posterior _p_ ( _**θ** |_ **x** ) for arbitrary **x** , LFI only tries to obtain an approximation of _p_ ( _**θ** |_ **x** _o_ ) for the given **x** _o_ . During the inference process, we can take advantage of the sampled data: _D_ = _{_ ( _**θ** i,_ **x** _i_ ) _}[n] i_ =1[where] **[x]** _[i][∼][p]_[(] **[x]** _[|]_ _**[θ]**[i]_[)][for][selected] values of _**θ** i_ .
**Biological black-box sequence design** . We consider biological sequence design as a black-box optimization problem:

where _f_ ( _·_ ) is the oracle score function, and we would like to discover values of **m** for which _f_ ( **m** ) is large. In real-world situations, a query of this oracle _f_ could represent a series of wet-lab experiments to measure specific chemical properties or specificity for a given binding site target. In general, these experiments are time- and cost-consuming. As a result, the total number of queries is limited.

In our setting, we use _M_ = _V[L]_ to denote the search space for sequences with fixed length _L_ , where _V_ is the vocabulary for each entry of the sequence: for DNA nucleotides _|V|_ = 4, and for protein amino acids _|V|_ = 20. For variable length setting, we have _M_ = _∪L∈_ [ _L_ min _,L_ max] _V[L]_ , where _L_ min and _L_ max are the minimal and maximal length, respectively.

## 2.2 CONNECTING LFI AND BLACK-BOX OPTIMIZATION

In order to draw a connection to LFI, we require a probabilistic formulation of the black-box sequence design problem. To this end, we relax the goal of searching for a single maximum of the oracle / score function _f_ to a posterior modeling problem, _i.e._ , finding a representative sample of the configurations of **m** sampled with probability related to some target posterior. Think of _C_ is the set of sequences with these desirable configurations, _E_ is a Boolean event about whether a sequence **m** belongs to _C_ , and our goal is to characterize the posterior distribution _p_ ( **m** _|E_ ) from which we obtain the desired sequences. Below, we consider two specific ways of doing this:

2

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

**Example A.** We explicitly define _C_ (and _E_ accordingly) as all the sequences whose scores are larger than a given threshold _s_ :

Here _s_ could be any fixed value, or a certain quantile of a particular score distribution. In this way, we have _p_ ( _E|_ **m** ) = _p_ ( **m** _∈C|_ **m** ) = 1 _{f_ ( **m** ) _≥ s}_ where 1 _{}_ is the indicator function.
**Example B.** In a softer version of _E_ and _C_ , we can define its conditional probability of being true to follow a Boltzmann distribution:

where _τ_ is a temperature parameter. We introduce the exponential because _f_ ( _·_ ) does not necessarily _·_ take positive values. Any monotone transformation of _f_ ( ) to non-negative reals could be used, so that sequences with larger oracle scores have a greater probability of making _E_ true.

With this posterior objective, our goal now becomes effectively modeling and sampling from the posterior _p_ ( **m** _|E_ ). It is thus natural to resort to the tools of Bayesian inference for this task. In order to examine this possibility, we draw a detailed comparison between the settings of black-box sequence design problem and likelihood-free Bayesian inference in Table 1.

|Likelihood-free inference
Black-box optimization|Likelihood-free inference
Black-box optimization|Likelihood-free inference
Black-box optimization|
|---|---|---|
||||
|Element|(**_θ_**_,_**x**)|(**m**_, s_)|
||||
|Target|_p_(**_θ_**_|_**x**_o_)|_p_(**m**_|E_)|
||||
|Constraint|limited simulation: **x**_∼p_(**x**_|_**_θ_**)
intractable likelihood: _p_(**x**_|_**_θ_**)|limited query: _s ∼f_(**m**)
black-box oracle: _f_(**m**)|

Table 1: Correspondence between likelihood-free inference and black-box optimization.

It can be observed that both tasks share similar elements and targets. The two settings also share similar limitations on the allowed queries, which are too time-consuming and / or cost-intensive. Notice that in sequence design, the oracle could be either exact or noisy, thus we use the more general _s ∼ f_ ( **m** ) formulation rather than _s_ = _f_ ( **m** ). We will further present several concrete examples as demonstrations of this correspondence in the following section.

Another way to understand this correspondence is to consider the following mapping _T_ :

Here we can see the score value _s_ as a quantitative metric for how close the generated data **x** (given _**θ**_ ) is to the target observed data **x** _o_ . In addition, querying the oracle in the sequence design setting can also be thought of as follows: (1) sample **x** _∼ p_ ( _·|_ _**θ**_ ) and then (2) calculate _s_ = _−∥_ **x** _−_ **x** _o∥_ under some distance _∥·∥_ . In this manner, _T_ could conceptually transform any LFI problem into a black-box optimization task. In this work, we only focus on the application of sequence design.

## 3 METHODOLOGY

We provide a recipe for designing new sequence design algorithms based on the correspondence in Section 2.2. The recipe induces different approaches by modeling different probabilistic component of the Bayesian inference problem. We begin with common algorithm restrictions under this setting.

**Common constraint for algorithms** . Due to the restriction of simulation / query in our setting, we constrain our algorithms to act in a sequential / iterative way, gradually achieving the desired posterior round by round. Every algorithm starts with an empty dataset _D_ = ∅ and an initial proposal _p_ 1( _·_ ) = _p_ ( _·_ ), where _p_ ( _·_ ) is the prior given by the task. In the _r_ -th round of this multi-round setting, the algorithm would use the proposal _pr_ ( _·_ ) of this round to sample a batch of data ( _**θ**_ / **m** ) for simulation / query, and augment the current dataset _D_ with the newly obtained batch of data. We use _n_ to denote the batch size for each round’s simulation / query. Afterwards, the algorithm updates

3

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

the proposal to _pr_ +1( _·_ ). The outcomes for the two settings we discuss may be slightly different: an algorithm for likelihood-free inference would return the posterior, while a sequence design method would return the dataset of all the sequences it has queried, which hopefully contains desired high scored sequences. On the other hand, a sequence design method could produce as an intermediate result a generative model for sampling queries, which then completely fits with the LFI framework.

## 3.1 BACKWARD MODELING OF THE MECHANISM

Approximate Bayesian Computation (ABC) (Beaumont et al., 2002) is a standard method for tackling LFI problems. In Algorithm 1, we display one of the most popular variants: Sequential Monte Carlo-Approximate Bayesian Computation (SMC-ABC) (Beaumont et al., 2009). In each round, parameters _**θ**_ are sampled from the current proposal distribution _pr_ ( _**θ**_ ) for simulation. A rejection step is then involved to remove the _**θ** i_ whose simulation outcomes **x** _i_ cannot reproduce the observed data **x** _o_ with sufficient accuracy. The remaining accepted _{_ _**θ** i}i_ are adopted to update the next round’s proposal _pr_ +1( _·_ ) towards the target posterior, _i.e._ , by refitting _qφ_ with the modified data. We defer more details of this approach to Section A.1 in Appendix.

It would then be natural to construct an analogical sequence design algorithm using top scored entities _{_ **m** _i}i_ to guide the update of a certain sequence distribution, see Algorithm 2. Interestingly, this is the proposed sequence design algorithm in Gupta & Zou (2019), where the authors name this kind of updating “feedback” because training of the parametric generator _qφ_ ( **m** ) exploits feedback signals from the oracle. In this paper, we follow Brookes & Listgarten (2018) to crystallize _qφ_ ( **m** ) to be a variational autoencoder (Kingma & Welling, 2014), and use the term Feedback-Variational AutoEncoder (FB-VAE) to refer to Algorithm 2. We place Algorithm 1 & 2 side-by-side to highlight their correspondence. We also make the same arrangement for the following Algorithm 3 & 4, Algorithm 5 & 6 and Algorithm 7 & 8.

In comparison with SMC-ABC, the Sequential Neural Posterior (SNP) method (Papamakarios & Murray, 2016; Lueckmann et al., 2017; Greenberg et al., 2019) for likelihood-free inference adopts a more flexible approach, taking the power of conditional neural density estimator ( _e.g._ , Papamakarios et al. (2017)) to model the general posterior _p_ ( _**θ** |_ **x** ), which takes arbitrary _**θ**_ and **x** as two inputs and outputs a distribution. This neural estimator is trained via approximately minimizing the

4

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

Kullback–Leibler (KL) divergence between _qφ_ ( _**θ** |_ **x** ) and the true posterior _p_ ( _**θ** |_ **x** ). We defer more training details to Section A.1 in Appendix. Under the connection viewpoint, one similar algorithm for sequence design is the Design by Adaptive Sampling (DbAS) proposed in Brookes & Listgarten (2018) which is characterized in Algorithm 4, fitting _qφ_ ( **m** ) through minimizing the KL divergence with the posterior _p_ ( **m** _|E_ ). Based on the difference in specific implementations, both algorithms have more than one variant, whose details are deferred to Section A.1 in Appendix.

We refer to the above algorithms as “backward modeling” because the trained generative network _qφ_ (going from **x** _/E_ to _**θ** /_ **m** ) is a sort of reverse model of the simulation mechanism (which goes from _**θ** /_ **m** to **x** _/s_ ).

## 3.2 FORWARD MODELING OF THE MECHANISM

Whereas the above methods focus on directly modeling the target posterior with a generative model that learns a “reverse mechanism” of the simulation process, it is also possible to model the “forward mechanism”, which is consistent with the simulation process. Papamakarios et al. (2019) claim that the forward modeling approach may be an easier task than its backward counterpart, as unbiased estimation of the likelihood does not depend on the choice of proposal. Consequently, in contrast to SNP, Papamakarios et al. (2019) chooses to train a neural density estimator to model the conditional likelihood distribution _qφ_ ( **x** _|_ _**θ**_ ) sequentially in each round. The training is achieved by maximizing the total log likelihood max _q_ � _i_[log] _[ q][φ]_[(] **[x]** _[i][|]_ _**[θ]**[i]_[)][ with data samples from the dataset] _[ D]_[at the current] ( _r_ -th) round. The downside of this forward approach is an additional computational Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) step is needed to sample from the _r_ -th round posterior / proposal _pr_ ( _**θ**_ ). The resulting approach, which is coined (Papamakarios et al., 2019) the Sequential Neural Likelihood (SNL), is summarized in Algorithm 5.

Inˆ the spirit of directly modeling the forward mechanism of sequence design, we train a regressor procedure of the proposal of the next round, we use _fφ_ ( **m** ) in a supervised manner to fit the oracle scorer. _q_ ˜(In order to adapt this regressor into the update **m** ) to denote the unknown posterior _p_ ( **m** _|E_ ) ofwith knowledge of _E_ . For instance, if we choose Example B in Section 2.2 to be the definition of _f_[ˆ] _φ_ ( **m** ) and prior _p_ ( **m** ). The specific construction of ˜ _q_ ( **m** ) depends on the choice _E_ , then _q_ ˜( **m** ) is the distribution with (unnormalized) probability _p_ ( **m** ) _·_ exp( _f_[ˆ] _φ_ ( **m** ) _/τ_ ). See Section A.2 in Appendix for more elaboration about this point. We then choose the update procedure of the proposal _pr_ +1( **m** ) to be analogical to that of SNL. We name this proposed algorithm to be Iterative Scoring (IS) to avoid confusion with likelihood-free inference algorithms. Furthermore, depending on different definition of _E_ , we use the name “IS-A” and “IS-B” for them in the following sections.

## 3.3 MODELING A PROBABILITY RATIO

In this subsection, we discuss yet another approach, through the estimation of a probability ratio. Gutmann & Hyv¨arinen (2010) proposes noise contrastive estimation as a statistical inference approach. This methodology turns a hard probability modeling problem into binary classification, which is considered easier to learn. In contrast to the aforementioned likelihood-free inference methods which rely on a form of density estimation to perform the task, Sequential Neural Ratio

5

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

(SNR) (Hermans et al., 2019) takes a similar approach as noise contrastive estimation. SNR adopts a classification approach to estimate the likelihood-to-evidence ratio _r_ ( _**θ** ,_ **x** ) = _p_ ( _**θ** |_ **x** ) _/p_ ( _**θ**_ ). SNR is summarized in Algorithm 7. Specifically, in each round, SNR fits a binary classifier _dφ_ ( _**θ** ,_ **x** ) _∈_ [0 _,_ 1] in the following manner:

See the proof in Section A.3 in Appendix. After training _d_ , SNR can obtain the posterior density value by _p_ ( _**θ** |_ **x** ) = _r[∗]_ ( _**θ** ,_ **x** ) _p_ ( _**θ**_ ). SNR mirrors SNL in that it samples the new proposal _pr_ +1( _**θ**_ ) without explicitly modeling the posterior.

On the other hand, we propose a sequence design algorithm analogous to SNR and named Iterative Ratio (IR), which estimates the probability ratio _r_ ( **m** ) = _p_ ( **m** _|E_ ) _/p_ ( **m** ) for the purpose of posterior sampling. IR first builds two datasets _D_ and _D[′]_ , corresponding to two different distributions _p_ ( **m** _|E_ ) and _p_ ( **m** ). We take a similar binary classification approach, whose training objective is min _d_ �� **m** _∈D_[[] _[−]_[log] _[ d]_[(] **[m]**[)] +][ �] **m** _∈D[′]_[[] _[−]_[log(1] _[ −][d]_[(] **[m]**[))]] �. The desired ratio is then obtained by _r_ ( **m** ) := _d_ ( **m** ) _/_ (1 _− d_ ( **m** )). Other components of IR follow the scheme of IS, and IR is schematized in Algorithm 8. We point out that IR does not have two variants as IS does, as the definition for _E_ in Example B is not usable because of the unknown normalizing constant for probability _p_ ( _E|_ **m** ). See Section A.3 in Appendix for more explanation.

Interestingly, a recent SNR-like work, EG-LF-MCMC (Begy & Schikuta, 2021), proposes to train the classifier on tuples of ( _**θ** , ϵ_ = _∥_ **x** _−_ **x** _o∥_ ) instead of ( _**θ** ,_ **x** ). This algorithm can be also seen as a more precise analogy of our Iterative Ratio in the LFI context, as we have pointed out in Section 2.2 that a conceptual link could be drawn between _s_ and _−∥_ **x** _−_ **x** _o∥_ .

## 3.4 COMPOSITE PROBABILISTIC METHODS

Building on the above analogies and framework, we move beyond the above algorithms in this subsection. The previous lines of approach – the direct posterior modeling methods in Section 3.1 and the indirect methods in Section 3.2 and 3.3 – both have their own advantages and disadvantages. The former methods may fail to get accurate inference result due to unmatched proposals, while the latter ones would need extra large amount of computation for the MCMC sampling process before obtaining accurate posterior samples, etc. Here we study composite algorithms that combine the aforementioned ingredients through the lens of our proposed unified framework. Our goal is to combine the strengths from both kinds of methods.

We first introduce Iterative Posterior Scoring (IPS) method illustrated in Algorithm 9. IPS also uses a neural network _f_[ˆ] _φ_ ( **m** ) to model the forward mechanism as IS does, and again we use _q_ ˜ here train a second parametrized modelto denote the target posterior _p_ ( **m** _q|Eψ_ ).to modelInstead _q_ ˜ofby minimizing the KL divergence between them.applying computational MCMC steps here, we Notice that the optimization of _qψ_ is restricted within a neural network parameterization family. As a result, in the next round, we can directly utilize _qψ_ ( **m** ) to serve as a flexible generative proposal of _pr_ +1( **m** ). Like IS, the IPS algorithm also has two different variants with regard to different choices of _E_ , we name them to be IPS-A and IPS-B. Two choices differ in the detailed construction of distribution _q_ ˜( **m** ).

In a similar spirit, we propose the Iterative Posterior Ratio (IPR) algorithm (see Algorithm 10). IPR exactly the same as in IPS:the priorlike IPS. IPR works similarly to IR, in that we also constructis close to the IR algorithm in many aspects, but also adopts a second neural network model _p_ ( **m** ) simply via _q_ ˜ _q_ ( **m** _ψ_ () **m** _←_ ) is also achieved via minimizing KL divergence with _rφ_ ( **m** ) _· p_ ( **m** ). Note that the usage of two models in IPR is not _q_ ˜( **m** ), taking advantage of _rqφ_ ˜(( **mm** _qψ_ )),( and **m** but) the training of model _dφ_ ( **m** ) is closer to that in IR rather than the _f_[ˆ] _φ_ ( **m** ) in IS. Another similarity between IPR and IR is that IPR also only has one variant, since the Example B is not applicable for this ratio modeling approach (see Section A.4 in Appendix for more details).

## 4 EXPERIMENTS

## 4.1 SETUP

In this section, we systematically evaluate the proposed methods and baselines on four different _in-silico_ biological sequence design benchmarks. In every round, we allow each algorithm to query the black-box oracle for a batch of _n_ sequences **m** _i_ to obtain their true scores _si_ , with _n_ = 100 for all experiments. The total number of rounds differs across different tasks.
We experiment with our six proposed methods: Iterative Scoring (-A/B) from Section 3.2, Iterative Ratio from Section 3.3, Iterative Posterior Scoring (-A/B) and Iterative Posterior Ratio from Sec-

tion 3.4. Apart from these proposed methods, we consider as baselines a battery of existing methods designed for batched black-box sequence design tasks: (1) Random, a method that randomly select proposal sequences at every round; (2) FB-VAE (Gupta & Zou, 2019) depicted in Section 3.1; (3) Evolution based (Brindle, 1980; Real et al., 2019) sequence design algorithm; and (4) DbAS (Brookes & Listgarten, 2018), described in Section 3.1.

We evaluate these sequence design algorithms by the average score of the top-10 and top-100 sequences in the resulting dataset _D_ at each round. We plot the average score curves with regard to the number of rounds. We also use the area under the curve as a scalar metric for sample efficiency to compare the methods being evaluated. Specifically, since the area depends on the choice of x-axis, we simply cumulate the scores of all rounds to calculate the area. The result could be non-positive, since the score can take negative values.

## 4.2 RESULTS

**Transcription factor binding sites (TfBind)** . Protein sequences that bind with DNA sequences to adjust their activity are called transcription factors. In Barrera et al. (2016), the authors measure the binding properties between a battery of transcription factors and all possible length-8 DNA sequences through biological experiments. Concretely, we choose 15 transcription factors to serve as 15 different tasks. For each transcription factor, the algorithm needs to search for sequences that maximize the corresponding binding activity score. The size of the search space is _|V|[L]_ = 4[8] = 65536. The number of total rounds is fixed to 10. For validation, we follow Angerm¨uller et al. (2020b) and use one task (ZNF200 ~~S~~ 265Y ~~R~~ 1) for hyperparameter selection. Then we test the algorithms’ performance on the other 14 held-out tasks.
Figure 1 displays a comparison for all ten methods on three of the chosen binding affinity tasks. We can observe that after 10 rounds, our proposed methods perform consistently better than the

Figure 2: Top score (y-axis) curves of different methods on 3 sequence design problems ( _left_ : UTR, _middle_ : AMP, _right_ : Fluo) with regard to the number of rounds.
Table 3: Comparison of the area under top-100 curves for UTR, AMP and Fluo benchmarks. Larger area means better sample efficiency.

backward modeling methods like DbAS and FB-VAE in terms of both Top-10 and Top-100 scores. Among all baselines, the evolution method is the strongest one, and it beats IR on some of the tasks (see complete results in Table 4 and 5 in Appendix). We also find that IS-A and IS-B increase top scores slightly faster than other methods, especially on PBX4 ~~R~~ EF ~~R~~ 2 and CRX ~~E~~ 80A ~~R~~ 1. This indicates that composite methods’ way of using parameterized models to replace computational procedures is not the optimal solution for small-scale tasks.

**5’ untranslated regions (UTR)** . The translation efficiency is mainly determined by the sequence of 5’ UTR (Alipanahi et al., 2015). In Sample et al. (2019), the authors create a library of gene sequences with ribosome loading level as labels. They further train a convolutional neural network with this library to predict the relationship between a 5’UTR sequence and the corresponding gene expression level. We use this neural network as an oracle for this benchmark. The length of the gene sequences is fixed to 50, and thus the size of the search space is 4[50] . For this 5’UTR benchmark, we also allow each algorithm to explore for 10 rounds. Figure 2 (left) shows that our proposed composite methods significantly outperform other methods on the UTR task. Different from the results on the TfBind task, forward modeling methods do not achieve the best performance.

**Antimicrobial peptides (AMP)** . Protein modeling has recently become a popular sub-area of machine learning research. We are tasked to generate AMP sequences, which are short protein sequences against multi-resistant pathogens. We train a binary classifier model to classify whether a short protein sequence belongs to AMP and defer the related details to Appendix. This is the only task we consider regarding sequence design with alterable lengths, where the length of sequences ranges from 12 to 60. Since each entry of protein sequence has _|V|_ = 20 different choices on amino acids, the size of search space is[�][60] _L_ =12[20] _[L]_[.][We][set][the][number][of][total][rounds][to][be][15][for][this] task. Figure 2 (middle) clearly shows that the performances of IPS-A and IPS-B dominate the AMP generation task, which demonstrates the effectiveness of our composite strategy.

9

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

**Fluorescence proteins (Fluo)** . As another protein engineering task, we consider the optimization over fluorescent proteins, which is a commonly used test bed of modern molecular biology. This task is similar to the AMP task introduced above, but its ground-truth measurement relies on a regressor. We use a pretrained model taken from Rao et al. (2019) to act as our task oracle, which is a regressor trained to predict log-fluorescence intensity value over approximately 52,000 protein sequences of length 238 (Sarkisyan et al., 2016). More concretely, the regressor is fit on a small neighborhood of parent green fluorescent protein, and is then evaluated on a more distant protein. The training data is derived from the naturally occurring GFP in _Aequorea victoria_ . The task Fluo’s search space is 20[238] and we set the number of rounds to 20. We demonstrate the Fluo results in Figure 2 (right), where we can see both composite methods and DbAS achieve much better results than other approaches. This might signify that for long sequence design tasks, backward modeling is superior to other modeling methods, which is not consistent with LFI (Papamakarios et al., 2019). We also summarize the sample efficiency results for the latter 3 benchmarks in Table 3 and 6, from which we can see that our proposed three composite methods perform remarkably promising results.

## 5 CONCLUSION

We propose a probabilistic framework that unifies likelihood-free inference and black-box optimization for designing biological sequences. This unified perspective enables us to design a variety of novel composite probabilistic sequence design methods combining the best of both worlds. Extensive experiments have demonstrated the benefits of the unified perspective. While the composite probabilistic methods usually outperform other baseline methods in most sequence design tasks we consider in this work, the key contribution of our paper is not just about the superiority of those composite methods, as different specific tasks might prefer different algorithmic configurations due to no free lunch theorem (Wolpert & Macready, 1997). Actually, we would like to attribute the strong performance to the unified probabilistic framework, which enables us to develop a richer algorithm pool, based on which we can design performant algorithms for particular sequence design tasks.

zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3-flash-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: The paper provides a conceptually strong unification of Likelihood-Free Inference (LFI) and black-box optimization (BBO), specifically for biological sequence design. By mapping objective thresholds to posterior targets, the authors successfully 'reinvent' existing methods and propose novel composite algorithms (IPS, IPR). The methodology is sound, and the empirical results across TfBind, UTR, AMP, and Fluorescence tasks demonstrate clear improvements. However, there are minor technical inconsistencies in the presentation of the optimization objectives (notably the confusion between loss minimization and density maximization) and some ambiguity regarding the specific parameterization of the 'Example B' posterior used in the experiments.

comments.title: Inconsistency in optimization objective direction
comments.quote: q_{\phi} \leftarrow \arg \min_{q} \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}}[D_{KL}(p(\theta|\mathbf{x}) \parallel q)]
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 3 (and similarly in Algorithm 4), the authors define the update as minimizing a KL divergence where the second argument is 'q'. While this is a standard variational objective, it is written as an assignment to the density estimator q. However, the standard SNP (Sequential Neural Posterior) approach involves maximizing the log-probability of the parameters under the model. The text says 'minimizing the KL divergence', but if q is the model, the objective usually implies fitting q to p. In the SNP context (Greenberg et al. 2019), this is typically implemented as a maximum likelihood task. The notation $D_{KL}(p \parallel q)$ is correctly a forward KL for density estimation, but the algorithmic box presents it as a functional search without specifying the parametric form or the sample-based loss approximation. Most importantly, 'min q' is slightly informal compared to 'min \phi'.

comments.title: Unspecified threshold/temperature parameters for IPS-A and IPS-B
comments.quote: The specific construction of $\tilde{q}(\mathbf{m})$ depends on the choice of $E$ ... we use the name 'IS-A' and 'IS-B' for them
comments.explanation: The paper defines Example A using a threshold $s$ and Example B using a temperature $\tau$. In the methodology, it is noted that IPS-A and IPS-B correspond to these examples. However, in the experiment section (Section 4), the authors do not specify what values of $s$ (e.g., which quantile) or $\tau$ were used for the benchmarks. Since the performance of these 'A' vs 'B' variants differs significantly in Figure 1 and 2, the lack of specific parameter settings makes the methods difficult to reproduce exactly without or searching through the appendix (which was not fully provided) or source code.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency in Algorithm 8/10
comments.quote: p_{r+1}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \propto r_{\phi}(\mathbf{m}) \cdot p(\mathbf{m})
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 8 (Iterative Ratio) and Algorithm 10 (IPR), the text defines the proposal for the next round. However, in the algorithm box for Iterative Ratio, the left-hand side of the update equation uses $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ (from the LFI notation) instead of $\mathbf{m}$ (the sequence design notation used in the rest of the BBO algorithms). Given that this is a sequence design algorithm (returning sequences in $D$), the use of $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ is an inconsistency with the 'Element' definition in Table 1.

comments.title: Questionable claim regarding MCMC necessity in SNL
comments.quote: The downside of this forward approach is an additional computational Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) step is needed to sample from the $r$-th round posterior / proposal $p_r(\boldsymbol{\theta})$.
comments.explanation: While it is true that $p(\theta | \mathbf{x}_o) \propto p(\mathbf{x}_o | \theta) p(\theta)$ often requires MCMC if the likelihood is a complex neural density (like a Flow), it is not strictly a requirement of the forward architecture itself, but rather a consequence of the lack of conjugacy. In small-scale or specific architectures, other sampling methods exist. More importantly, Algorithm 6 (Iterative Scoring) seemingly solves this by 'constructing' $\tilde{q}$ and sampling from it, but the paper doesn't clarify if IS-A/B also requires MCMC for the internal proposal sampling or if it uses a simpler heuristic. This makes the comparison of 'computational burden' between SNL and IS slightly lopsided in the text.

zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Zero Shot (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents a compelling and well-structured framework that unifies likelihood-free inference and black-box optimization, particularly in the context of biological sequence design. The authors successfully draw meaningful analogies between established LFI methods and sequence optimization algorithms, and their extension to composite methods is both creative and well-motivated. The experimental evaluation is thorough, covering multiple benchmarks, and the results generally support the claims. However, there are several technical and logical issues related to consistency in notation, algorithmic formulation, and interpretation of probabilistic constructions that reduce confidence in the precise implementation and theoretical grounding of some proposed methods. While most issues are moderate in severity, one major concern affects the definition of a core algorithm, which could impact reproducibility and correctness.

comments.title: Inconsistent update of proposal in Algorithm 8
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 8 (Iterative Ratio), the line updating the proposal uses **_θ** in the left-hand side (_pr_+1(**_θ_**)) but the right-hand side depends on **m** and rφ(**m**). This is inconsistent in terms of variable naming and likely a typo. Given the context, the proposal should be over **m**, not **θ**. While the surrounding discussion correctly refers to the sequence **m**, the algorithm uses **θ** in the update step, which contradicts the rest of the algorithm’s variables. This is not just a notation issue—it could mislead a reader implementing the algorithm. The most plausible fix is that the left-hand side should be _pr_+1(**m**), consistent with other algorithms. Given the centrality of this step, the error could affect correct reproduction of the method.

comments.title: Ambiguous construction of ˜D′ in Algorithm 8 and IR
comments.quote: construct ˜_D′_ from_p_(**m**);
comments.explanation: The instruction to 'construct ˜D′ from p(**m**)' is underspecified. In the context of noise contrastive estimation and SNR, D′ typically consists of samples from the proposal distribution paired with simulated data. Here, ˜D′ should presumably be a set of sequences sampled from the prior p(**m**) and labeled as negative examples. However, the size of ˜D′, the sampling mechanism, and whether it's resampled each round are not specified. In contrast, Algorithm 7 clearly samples **θ′_i ∼ pr(**θ**), suggesting that ˜D′ should be sampled from the current proposal _pr_(**m**), not the prior _p_(**m**). This mismatch is critical because using the prior instead of the evolving proposal could lead to poor classifier calibration. The text says 'construct ˜D′ from p(**m**)', but this contradicts the analogy to SNR, where negative samples come from the current proposal. This ambiguity affects reproducibility and theoretical validity.

comments.title: Incorrect posterior construction in forward modeling via temperature scaling
comments.quote: then _q_ ˜( **m** ) is the distribution with (unnormalized) probability _p_ ( **m** ) _·_ exp( _f_[ˆ] _φ_ ( **m** ) _/τ_ )
comments.explanation: The construction of ˜q(**m**) as _p_(**m**) · exp(ˆf_φ(**m**)/τ) assumes that ˆf_φ(**m**) estimates f(**m**), and that p(E|**m**) ∝ exp(f(**m**)/τ) as defined in Example B. However, in that example, p(E|**m**) = exp(f(**m**)/τ) / Z(**m**,τ), which is not a normalized likelihood. The posterior p(**m**|E) ∝ p(**m**) p(E|**m**) ∝ p(**m**) exp(f(**m**)/τ), so the form is correct *if* ˆf_φ(**m**) is a good estimate of f(**m**). However, the regression step in Algorithm 6 trains ˆf_φ(**m**) to predict s_i ≈ f(**m_i**), so in expectation it targets f(**m**). The issue arises in the temperature scaling: τ is not estimated but treated as a hyperparameter. The paper does not clarify how τ is chosen or whether it should be scaled with the range of f. This leads to potential mismatch—using a fixed τ when f varies across tasks could distort the posterior. While not an outright error, the lack of discussion on τ selection is a notable gap in the method specification.

comments.title: Mismatch in KL minimization direction in Algorithm 4 and 9
comments.quote: _qφ ←_arg min_q D_KL(_p_(**m**_|E_)_∥q_);
comments.explanation: The KL divergence D_KL(p( **m**|E ) ∥ q) is minimized over q, which is the standard formulation for variational inference when matching a target posterior. However, this requires knowing or sampling from p(**m**|E), which is exactly the unknown distribution we are trying to approximate. In practice, likelihood-free methods like SNL or SNP use a reverse KL or alternative objectives that are tractable. The forward KL (data-generating perspective) is intractable unless samples from the posterior are available. In Algorithm 4, the dataset D contains samples from pr(**m**), not from p(**m**|E), so it's unclear how one would compute or approximate this KL. The text mentions deferring to Appendix A.1, but based on standard practice, this is likely a typo and should be the reverse KL D_KL(q ∥ p(**m**|E)) or an amortized version using simulation data. As written, the objective is not computable, making the algorithm ill-defined.

comments.title: Inconsistent use of temperature in Boltzmann definition
comments.explanation: In Example B, the paper defines p(E|**m**) = exp(f(**m**)/τ) / Z, but earlier states that any monotonic transformation to non-negative reals could be used. However, the exponential form imposes a very specific parametric assumption. More importantly, no justification is provided for dividing f(**m**) by τ rather than scaling it multiplicatively or applying a sigmoid. In standard energy-based models, τ controls the sharpness, but here f(**m**) could be unbounded or negative, and without normalization, the resulting probabilities could be ill-behaved. While the use of τ is common, the claim that 'any monotonic transformation' could be used contradicts the specific exponential form, which is not closed under monotonic transforms. This creates ambiguity about what flexibility actually exists in defining E.

comments.title: Ambiguous batch indexing in algorithm loops
comments.quote: **until**_n_samples are obtained
comments.explanation: Throughout the algorithms, the loop structure says 'repeat... until n samples are obtained', but no rejection condition is specified in IS, IR, IPS, or IPR. In SMC-ABC (Algorithm 1), samples are accepted based on ∥x_i − x_o∥, but in forward modeling variants (Algorithms 6, 8, 9, 10), no such filtering is mentioned. This suggests that all n samples are always accepted, making the repeat-until loop redundant—it could be replaced with a simple for loop. The phrasing might carry over from ABC, but in the absence of rejection, it's misleading. A reader might incorrectly assume that some samples are rejected based on score threshold, but the text does not support this. While likely just imprecise language, it could confuse interpretation of the sampling mechanism.

comments.title: Missing justification for classifier-based ratio estimation in IR
comments.quote: The desired ratio is then obtained by _r_ ( **m** ) := _d_ ( **m** ) _/_ (1 _− d_ ( **m** ))
comments.explanation: The paper asserts this form without derivation, referencing Proposition 1 which applies to the ratio p(**θ**|x)/p(**θ**) via a classifier on (**θ**,x). However, in IR, the classifier is trained only on **m**, not on (**m**,s) or any auxiliary variable. The standard result requires the classifier to distinguish data from two joint distributions: p(**m**, E) and p(**m**)p(E|**m**) vs. p(**m**)p_neg(E). Here, ˜D is constructed from sequences satisfying E, but E itself depends on f(**m**) ≥ s or a soft version—yet the classifier sees only **m**, not s or any score. This breaks the analogy to SNR, where the classifier sees both **θ** and **x**. Without including the score or an auxiliary variable, the classifier may not reliably estimate the density ratio. The method may still work empirically, but the theoretical motivation is weakened. This is a gap in connecting the algorithm to the foundational ratio estimation theory.

zero_shot__glm-4.6.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.6)
zero_shot__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: The paper presents a compelling unified probabilistic framework connecting likelihood-free inference and black-box optimization for biological sequence design. The theoretical connections are well-motivated and the correspondence between LFI and sequence design is clearly established. The proposed composite algorithms (IPS, IPR) represent a thoughtful synthesis of forward and backward modeling approaches. The experiments across four diverse benchmarks demonstrate the practical benefits of this unified perspective. However, there are some methodological ambiguities that need clarification, particularly regarding the implementation details of certain algorithms.

comments.title: Underspecified construction of dataset in Algorithm 8
comments.quote: construct ˜D with m in D satisfying E
comments.explanation: The algorithm for Iterative Ratio (IR) states to construct ˜D with sequences from D that satisfy event E, but doesn't specify how this is done for different definitions of E. For Example A (hard threshold), this is straightforward - select sequences with f(m) ≥ s. However, for Example B (Boltzmann distribution), E is probabilistic and it's unclear how to determine which sequences 'satisfy' E. The authors later mention that IR doesn't have two variants because Example B is not usable, but this critical restriction should be explicitly stated in the algorithm description or immediately after, to prevent readers from attempting to apply IR with Example B's definition.

comments.title: Ambiguous construction of posterior in Algorithm 6
comments.quote: construct ˜q(m) with ˆfφ(·) and p(m)
comments.explanation: In the Iterative Scoring algorithm, the construction of ˜q(m) is not fully specified within the algorithm box. While the text in Section 3.2 explains that for Example B, ˜q(m) has unnormalized probability p(m)·exp(ˆfφ(m)/τ), this crucial detail is missing from the algorithm itself. A reader implementing the algorithm from the pseudocode alone would not know how to construct ˜q(m). This should be explicitly stated in the algorithm or referenced clearly.

comments.title: Missing discussion of temperature parameter sign
comments.quote: where τ is a temperature parameter. We introduce the exponential because f(·) does not necessarily · take positive values.
comments.explanation: The authors introduce the exponential transformation in Example B to handle potentially negative values of f(·), but don't discuss the sign of the temperature parameter τ. If τ were negative, the exponential would invert the ordering, making sequences with lower scores have higher probability. While τ is typically assumed positive in Boltzmann distributions, this should be explicitly stated to avoid any ambiguity.

zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback: The paper provides a compelling and well-structured unification of likelihood-free inference (LFI) and black-box optimization (BBO). By mapping LFI components to BBO elements, the authors successfully derive existing techniques as special cases and introduce novel composite algorithms. The technical presentation is clear, and the experimental validation covers a diverse range of biological sequence design tasks. The primary issues identified involve minor notation inconsistencies and a potential ambiguity in the definition of the acquisition functions for the proposed composite methods, which are common in bridging two distinct fields. Overall, the logic is sound and the contribution is significant.

comments.title: Notation consistency in the definition of ˜q(m)
comments.quote: tilde q(m) to denote the unknown posterior p(m|E) of with knowledge of E. For instance, if we choose Example B in Section 2.2 to be the definition of E, then tilde q(m) is the distribution with (unnormalized) probability p(m) · exp(fˆφ(m)/τ).
comments.explanation: In Section 3.2, the authors use `˜q(m)` to denote a target posterior or its unnormalized form. However, later in Section 3.4 (Algorithm 9 and Algorithm 10), the variable `qψ` is introduced as a parametrized model (neural network) to approximate this `˜q(m)`. While the transition is contextually understandable, the text in 3.4 is slightly garbled due to OCR artifacts (e.g., 'we use q ˜ here train a second parametrized model'), which makes the precise definition of the target optimization objective (the KL divergence between the unnormalized `˜q` and the parametrized surrogate `qψ`) difficult to parse for a reader who is not already an expert in the underlying LFI literature.

comments.title: Ambiguity in loss function derivation for IPR
comments.quote: min d [sum_{m ∈ D} [ -log d(m) ] + sum_{m ∈ D'} [ -log(1 - d(m)) ] ]
comments.explanation: The loss function provided for training the classifier `dφ` in the IR/IPR section is missing the standard normalization factors (1/|D| and 1/|D'|) typically found in empirical risk minimization for binary classification. While this is a standard omission in some informal writing, it clashes with the rigorous tone elsewhere in the paper. The reader must infer that the authors intend the empirical average, which is necessary for the classification loss to converge to the desired probability ratio.

comments.title: Notation clash: “*” symbol usage
comments.quote: Specifically, in each round, SNR fits a binary classifier dφ(θ, x) ∈ [0, 1] ... where the “*” denotes the optimality. We have the following Proposition 1:
comments.explanation: The author uses the 'superscript *' to denote both the optimal function (e.g., d*) and at the end of section 3.3 to refer to related work (e.g., EG-LF-MCMC). While the context distinguishes these uses, using the same symbol in such close proximity to describe both a mathematical variable state (optimality) and a footnote-style indicator is slightly distracting.

zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Zero Shot (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper presents a novel unified probabilistic framework connecting likelihood-free inference (LFI) and black-box sequence design, proposing several composite algorithms (IPS, IPR) that empirically outperform baselines on biological sequence benchmarks. The conceptual mapping and algorithmic parallels are well-motivated and the experimental evaluation is systematic. However, the manuscript contains several notation inconsistencies in the pseudocode (swapping variables between LFI and sequence design domains), a significant omission of the target observation in the SNR algorithm, and two heavily garbled paragraphs due to PDF/OCR artifacts that obscure methodological descriptions. Additionally, a theoretical claim regarding the inapplicability of the Boltzmann distribution to ratio-based methods is misleading, as density ratios inherently cancel normalizing constants. With careful revision of the notation, clarification of ranking conventions, and rewriting of the corrupted sections, the paper will be highly reproducible and robust.

zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Zero Shot (deepseek-v4-flash)
zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper presents an interesting conceptual unification of likelihood-free inference and black-box optimization for biological sequence design, and proposes several algorithmic variants. However, several critical implementation details are lacking, which undermines reproducibility. Specifically, the handling of unnormalized proposals for the forward modeling and ratio-based methods (IS, IR) is not described: the algorithms state 'p_{r+1}(m) ← q̃(m)' without mentioning how to sample from this unnormalized distribution, despite noting that MCMC is required for the analogous LFI method. Additionally, the threshold for indicator-based event definitions (Example A) is left unspecified for IS-A, IR, IPS-A, and IPR. A typo in Algorithm 8 mixes up variables. These gaps should be addressed to fully realize the paper's potential.

comments.title: Missing MCMC sampling step for Iterative Scoring and Iterative Ratio
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 6 (Iterative Scoring), the proposal for the next round is set directly to q̃(m), which is defined (for Example B) as an unnormalized distribution proportional to p(m)·exp(f̂_φ(m)/τ). The paper earlier acknowledges that for the analogous SNL method an additional MCMC step is needed to sample from such an unnormalized posterior. However, for IS the algorithm description omits this crucial step. The same issue applies to Algorithm 8 (Iterative Ratio), where p_{r+1}(·) is given as proportional to r_φ(m)·p(m) without any sampling procedure. Without specifying how samples are drawn from these unnormalized targets, the methods cannot be reproduced as presented.

comments.title: Threshold for indicator-based event definition not specified
comments.quote: construct D̃ with m in D satisfying E
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 8 (Iterative Ratio) and similarly for IS-A, IPS-A, etc., the event E is defined via Example A as {m: f(m) ≥ s}. However, the threshold s is never specified. It could be a fixed value or a quantile of the scores in the current dataset, but this choice critically affects which sequences are labeled as 'positive' for classifier training. Without this detail, the methods are underspecified. The same omission occurs for the construction of q̃(m) in IS-A.

comments.title: Variable mismatch in Algorithm 8
comments.quote: pr+1(θ) ∝ rφ(m)·p(m)
comments.explanation: The update line in Algorithm 8 uses θ on the left-hand side (p_{r+1}(θ)) while the right-hand side involves m and p(m). This is a clear typo; it should read p_{r+1}(m) ∝ r_φ(m)·p(m). The previous lines consistently use m for sequences, so the θ appears to be a leftover from the LFI template. This does not affect understanding but should be corrected.

comments.title: Unnumbered equation reference in Section 3.3
comments.quote: with the loss in Eq. 4
comments.explanation: The text refers to 'Eq. 4' but no equation number 4 is defined in the paper. The loss function for the classifier is described informally in the preceding sentences. This is a minor formatting issue that could confuse readers looking for a numbered equation.

zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.7-flash)
zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper proposes a valuable unified probabilistic framework for connecting likelihood-free inference (LFI) and black-box optimization (BBO), specifically applied to biological sequence design. The overall approach is theoretically sound and demonstrates practical benefits through composite methods. However, there is a significant discrepancy in the formal description of the 'Design by Adaptive Sampling' (DbAS) algorithm (Algorithm 4) and related LFI analogs where the update mechanism relies on conditioning on an observed data point $x_o$, which is inconsistent with the optimization goal of maximizing scores. Additionally, the paper makes an overstatement regarding the universal necessity of MCMC for forward modeling methods, which is not true for all modern implementations.

comments.title: Incorrect update logic for Design by Adaptive Sampling (Algo 4) compared to Section 2.2
comments.quote: fitting $q\phi$ (m) through minimizing the KL divergence with the posterior p(m|E) \[Equation Box: $q\phi \leftarrow$ arg min$_q$ E$_x$ [ D_KL(p(\theta|x) || q) ]; $p_{r+1}(\theta) \leftarrow q\phi(\theta|x_o)$]
comments.explanation: The text explicitly states the goal is to fit the posterior $p(m|E)$, where $E$ represents an event (e.g., 'score > threshold'). However, the formal equation minimizes the KL divergence between $q$ and $p(m|x)$ (where $x$ likely represents the observed scores $s_i$ from the dataset $D$), and subsequently updates the proposal to $q(m|x_o)$. In the context of sequence design optimization, there is typically no single observed data point $x_o$ (a specific sequence property value) to condition on when the goal is to maximize scores. Instead, one must update the proposal towards the region of high scores (the event $E$). Algorithm 6 (Iterative Scoring) correctly addresses this by constructing $	ilde{q}(m)$ from the regressor and prior. Algorithm 4, as written, implies fitting the model to match a specific score $x_o$, which is regression, not optimization, and breaks the analogy with the Section 2.2 definition of the target posterior.

comments.title: Overstated computational requirements for forward modeling (SNL)
comments.quote: The downside of this forward approach is an additional computational Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) step is needed to sample from the r-th round posterior / proposal
comments.explanation: The authors claim that the forward modeling approach (specifically Sequential Neural Likelihood) requires an MCMC step for sampling, casting it as a drawback. However, this limitation does not hold for all implementations of the forward approach. Recent advancements (e.g., Papamakarios et al., 2019) propose using Normalizing Flows as conditional density estimators. Flows are explicitly designed to be invertible (sampler) and differentiable (estimator), thereby allowing exact sampling from the posterior without requiring MCMC. Consequently, the statement is an overstatement that may mislead readers regarding the computational complexity of the general class of methods being unified.
